title: The Mother Beneath the Cross
narration: I stand below him, and his eyes search for mine.
The blood has poured out of him; the veins are emptied, the strength is gone.
I watch the eyes I knew grow heavy, begin to fail.
I watch the face I kissed turn pale.
Now he bends his head toward me.
Toward me. His mother.
And I know what this bowing is. It is a last greeting.
An ave he cannot speak, for the pain is past all words.
Ave of grief, ave of every desolation — he gives it with his head alone.
He commends his body to me. This body, so cruelly dug and torn, broken on every side.
I bore this body. I held it whole. Now I am given it ruined.
He turns also toward his Father, showing he dies obeying, not unwilling.
Even thanking him — that the Father calls him home.
He teaches us, even now, to give thanks in our suffering.
Then the great cry. He summons all his voice, and weeping, calls out.
Father, into your hands I commend my spirit.
And with his head bowed, he gives up the spirit — not torn from him, but sent.
When he willed it, and because he willed it, he breathes his last.
The cry was so great it was heard down into the depths; heaven and earth tremble.
No dying man could cry like that. This was the voice of God.
I feel the ground move beneath my feet, and I do not fall.
Of all his pains, this parting is the bitterest, for nothing is more terrible than death.
And I, his mother, stand. I stand, and I receive him.
prayer: who in the ninth hour, hanging on the gibbet,
cried with a great voice and into the Father's hands commended your spirit,
and with bowed head gave that same spirit up:
keep my spirit, I pray, commended to you now and always.
Pierce my heart through with the sword of love,
press into me the wounds of your body,
and at the last, set my spirit, commended into your hands,

latinJewel.latin: Pater, in manus tuas commendo spiritum meum.
latinJewel.english: Father, into your hands I commend my spirit.
